subtopic_detail: Routine booking bloods - which test panel
stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 woman attends her general practice for a first antenatal visit at 9 weeks gestation by certain last menstrual period and a confirmatory transvaginal scan showing a single intrauterine pregnancy with foetal cardiac activity. She has no significant medical history, takes only a pregnancy multivitamin containing 500 micrograms of folic acid daily, and has no known drug allergies. Her partner is well and there is no family history of inheritable disease. She was born in Melbourne, has no recent overseas travel, and reports she received routine childhood and adolescent vaccinations through her local council clinic but no longer has the records. She has never been pregnant before and has never had a blood transfusion. Her body mass index is 24 kg/m2, blood pressure is 112/68 mmHg, and urinalysis on a midstream sample today shows no nitrites, leucocytes, protein, blood or glucose. She has not had any screening blood tests since adolescence. She asks the general practitioner which blood tests are routinely offered at this first visit under RANZCOG and state maternity guidance.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/68 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.7 degC, BMI 24 kg/m2.
data_table.Examination: Cardiorespiratory and abdominal examination unremarkable. No clinical pallor or lymphadenopathy.
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick negative. Confirmatory TV ultrasound: single intrauterine pregnancy, CRL consistent with 9+1 weeks, foetal heart rate 168 bpm.

lead_in: Which set of investigations most accurately reflects the standard Australian first-trimester routine antenatal booking panel for this woman?

options.text: FBC, blood group and antibody screen, rubella IgG, syphilis serology, HIV serology, hepatitis B surface antigen, hepatitis C antibody, MSU MCS, plus an HPV cervical screen if due
options.rationale: RANZCOG and state maternity policies (e.g. NSW Health, KEMH) recommend FBC, blood group + antibody screen, rubella IgG, syphilis, HIV, HBsAg, HCV antibody, and MSU MCS at booking, with cervical screening if due (RANZCOG; NSW Health Maternity - Antenatal Care).

options.text: FBC, blood group and antibody screen, rubella IgG, syphilis serology, HIV serology, hepatitis B surface antigen, hepatitis C antibody, MSU MCS, plus routine TORCH IgM panel for all women
options.rationale: Routine universal TORCH IgM screening is not recommended in low-risk pregnancies; it has poor positive predictive value and generates unnecessary anxiety. TORCH serology is targeted to clinical suspicion or exposure (RANZCOG).

options.text: FBC, blood group and antibody screen, rubella IgG, syphilis serology, hepatitis B surface antigen, MSU MCS, with HIV serology only offered if injecting drug use or other risk factors are disclosed
options.rationale: HIV serology is now offered universally at antenatal booking in Australia, not risk-based, to prevent vertical transmission via timely antiretroviral therapy (RANZCOG; ASHM perinatal HIV guidance).

options.text: FBC, blood group and antibody screen, rubella IgG, varicella IgG, syphilis serology, HIV serology, hepatitis B surface antigen, hepatitis C antibody, MSU MCS, and a 75 g OGTT today
options.rationale: Universal varicella IgG and routine first-trimester 75 g OGTT are not part of the standard booking panel; OGTT is offered at 24 to 28 weeks (or earlier only for high-risk women) and varicella serology is targeted by history (RANZCOG; ADIPS).

options.text: FBC, blood group and antibody screen, ferritin, vitamin D, TSH, syphilis serology, HIV serology, hepatitis B surface antigen, hepatitis C antibody, and combined first-trimester screening today
options.rationale: Universal first-trimester TSH and vitamin D are not core booking tests in Australia; they are targeted by risk. Combined first-trimester screening is offered between 11+0 and 13+6 weeks, not at 9 weeks (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

explanation.summary: The Australian first-trimester booking blood panel covers maternal anaemia, blood group/antibody status, rubella immunity, and infections relevant to vertical transmission or treatable in pregnancy (syphilis, HIV, hepatitis B and C). MSU MCS detects asymptomatic bacteriuria. Aneuploidy and GDM screening are offered later in pregnancy windows.
key_points: Core booking bloods: FBC, blood group + antibody screen, rubella IgG, syphilis, HIV, HBsAg, HCV antibody
MSU MCS for asymptomatic bacteriuria; dipstick alone misses up to 50%
Cervical screening if due (HPV-based, every 5 years from age 25)
Vitamin D, TSH, ferritin, varicella IgG, OGTT are targeted/risk-based or done later
Combined first-trimester screen 11+0 to 13+6 weeks; NIPT may be done from 10 weeks
explanation.pearls: Offer (do not impose) HIV and hepatitis screening to every woman; opt-out, not risk-based, since the era of antiretroviral prophylaxis.

sources.label: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

subtopic_detail: Combined first-trimester screen - next-step after high risk
stem: A 36-year-old G2P1 woman attends her general practitioner with the result of her combined first-trimester screen, performed at 12 weeks and 4 days gestation with the recommended ultrasound markers (crown-rump length 62 mm, nuchal translucency 3.2 mm, present nasal bone) and maternal serum PAPP-A and free beta-hCG. The laboratory adjusted risk for trisomy 21 has come back as 1 in 80, with trisomy 18 risk 1 in 950 and trisomy 13 risk 1 in 1100. Her first child is well at age 4 with no chromosomal abnormality. There is no family history of aneuploidy or congenital structural malformations. Her booking bloods are unremarkable. She and her partner have read about NIPT and diagnostic testing online and want to know the most appropriate immediate next step. She is keen to avoid procedure-related miscarriage risk if reasonable. The pregnancy is otherwise progressing well, foetal heart was 162 bpm on a brief ultrasound today and she has no bleeding, pain or symptoms of concern.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 80 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Soft, non-tender abdomen. Uterus not yet palpable abdominally.
data_table.Investigations: cFTS at 12+4: NT 3.2 mm, nasal bone present, PAPP-A 0.4 MoM, free beta-hCG 2.8 MoM. Adjusted T21 risk 1 in 80, T18 risk 1 in 950, T13 risk 1 in 1100.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in counselling and investigation for this woman?

options.text: Reassure that the cFTS result represents a low-risk pregnancy and proceed to a routine 18 to 20 week morphology scan
options.rationale: A risk of 1 in 80 is well above the high-risk threshold (commonly 1 in 300 in Australian laboratories) and an NT >=3.0 mm independently warrants further evaluation; this is not low risk (RANZCOG Prenatal screening).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Repeat the combined first-trimester screen at 13+6 weeks to see if the risk falls before any further action is taken
options.rationale: cFTS is a single screening event; repeating it is not standard, will not move risk meaningfully, and delays definitive evaluation (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Proceed directly to chorionic villus sampling today without further screening, since maternal age above 35 makes diagnostic testing mandatory
options.rationale: Maternal age alone is no longer an indication for invasive diagnostic testing; risk is individualised by cFTS or NIPT, and the woman should be offered choice including a non-invasive contingent test (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Offer NIPT as a contingent screening test, or diagnostic testing (CVS now or amniocentesis from 15 weeks) after counselling about procedure-related miscarriage risk
options.rationale: RANZCOG recommends that after a high-risk cFTS the woman is offered counselling about both contingent NIPT (high sensitivity for T21) and definitive diagnostic testing (CVS from about 10 weeks or amniocentesis from 15 weeks). NIPT remains a screening test - a positive must be confirmed diagnostically. Procedure-related miscarriage risk for CVS and amniocentesis is approximately 0.1 to 0.2% in experienced units (RANZCOG; HGSA).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing
HGSA/RANZCOG Joint statement on prenatal screening

options.text: Arrange amniocentesis today at 12+4 weeks, given the high-risk result and the woman's preference to avoid further screening tests
options.rationale: Amniocentesis is not performed before 15 completed weeks because of higher procedure-related foetal loss and limb defect risk; CVS is the diagnostic option in the first trimester (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

explanation.summary: A high-risk combined first-trimester screen (commonly >=1 in 300) should trigger counselling about contingent NIPT or definitive diagnostic testing. CVS is available from about 10 weeks; amniocentesis from 15 weeks. NIPT is a screening test - positive results require diagnostic confirmation.
key_points: cFTS window: 11+0 to 13+6 weeks (CRL 45 to 84 mm)
High-risk threshold typically 1 in 300 in Australian labs
NT >=3.5 mm independently increases risk of aneuploidy AND congenital heart disease - foetal echo at 20 weeks
NIPT sensitivity ~99% for T21, ~97% for T18, lower for sex chromosome aneuploidies
Procedure-related miscarriage CVS/amnio ~0.1 to 0.2% in experienced units
explanation.pearls: Never report an NIPT positive result as diagnostic - always confirm with CVS or amniocentesis before any irreversible decision.

sources.label: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing
HGSA/RANZCOG Joint statement on prenatal screening

stem: A 41-year-old G3P2 woman attends a tertiary maternal foetal medicine clinic at 13 weeks and 2 days gestation. She declined combined first-trimester screening and instead self-funded non-invasive prenatal testing at 11 weeks. The result has been telephoned through by her general practitioner this morning as a high-probability positive for trisomy 21, with foetal fraction reported at 8.2% and no comment on sex chromosome or other autosomal findings. Trisomy 18 and 13 results were low risk. Her two previous children were chromosomally normal. She has hypothyroidism on thyroxine 100 micrograms daily, has had a previous lower-segment caesarean for breech, and there is no significant family history of inheritable disease. Her booking bloods were unremarkable. An ultrasound today shows a viable singleton pregnancy with CRL consistent with dates, a nuchal translucency of 2.1 mm, present nasal bone, no other structural concerns, and a normally implanted placenta on the posterior uterine wall. She is distressed and asks the maternal foetal medicine consultant for clear advice on what the NIPT result actually means and what the next step should be.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen. Uterus 13-14 week size.
data_table.Investigations: NIPT at 11 weeks: high probability trisomy 21, foetal fraction 8.2%, T18/T13 low risk. Today's USS: viable singleton, CRL 70 mm, NT 2.1 mm, nasal bone present, no structural anomalies, posterior placenta.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step for counselling and management?

options.text: Counsel that NIPT has very high positive predictive value at her age and proceed directly to termination of pregnancy if she wishes, without further confirmatory testing
options.rationale: Even at advanced maternal age, NIPT is a screening test, not diagnostic. False positives occur (confined placental mosaicism, vanishing twin, maternal mosaicism, maternal malignancy). Irreversible decisions should not be made on screening results alone (RANZCOG; HGSA).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing
HGSA/RANZCOG Joint statement on prenatal screening

options.text: Proceed directly to amniocentesis today at 13+2 weeks for the most accurate confirmatory result
options.rationale: Amniocentesis before 15 completed weeks is associated with higher foetal loss and talipes; CVS is the correct first-trimester diagnostic test. Amniocentesis is an option if she prefers to wait until 15+ weeks (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Repeat the NIPT on a fresh maternal sample to confirm the result before any consideration of diagnostic testing
options.rationale: Repeating NIPT does not resolve the screening/diagnostic distinction - the same biological mechanisms producing the positive (e.g. confined placental mosaicism) will produce the same result. Diagnostic testing is required (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Reassure her because the nuchal translucency today is normal at 2.1 mm and there are no structural anomalies; defer further testing to the 20 week morphology scan
options.rationale: A normal NT and morphology scan reduce but do not exclude trisomy 21; up to 50% of T21 foetuses have normal NT and ultrasound is not diagnostic. Definitive testing is required to confirm or refute the NIPT result (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Counsel that NIPT remains a screening test and offer confirmatory diagnostic testing by chorionic villus sampling now, or amniocentesis from 15 weeks, before any irreversible decision is made
options.rationale: RANZCOG and HGSA both emphasise that a positive NIPT requires diagnostic confirmation by CVS or amniocentesis before any decisions about pregnancy continuation. PPV for T21 with high-probability NIPT is high in older women but not 100%, and false positives occur through placental mosaicism, vanishing twin, maternal copy number variants and rarely maternal malignancy. CVS from 10 to 13+6 weeks is the earliest diagnostic option (RANZCOG; HGSA).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing
HGSA/RANZCOG Joint statement on prenatal screening

explanation.summary: A positive NIPT is a high-probability screening result that must be confirmed by diagnostic testing (CVS or amniocentesis) before any irreversible decision. Foetal fraction above 4% is technically adequate; the test still has false positives through confined placental mosaicism, vanishing twin and maternal causes.
key_points: NIPT sensitivity ~99% for T21, specificity ~99.9%; PPV varies with prior risk
Foetal fraction below 4% is a no-call/low-confidence result; 8.2% is adequate
Causes of false positive NIPT: confined placental mosaicism, vanishing twin, maternal CNV, rarely maternal malignancy
CVS 10 to 13+6 weeks; amniocentesis from 15 weeks
Procedure-related miscarriage 0.1 to 0.2% in experienced units
explanation.pearls: When telephoning a positive NIPT result, always say 'high-probability screening positive - we need a diagnostic test to confirm'. Never imply diagnosis.

sources.label: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing
HGSA/RANZCOG Joint statement on prenatal screening

subtopic_detail: Diagnostic testing - CVS vs amniocentesis timing and risk
stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 woman is reviewed in a maternal foetal medicine clinic at 11 weeks and 5 days gestation by certain dates and a confirmatory ultrasound. Her combined first-trimester screen returned a risk of 1 in 60 for trisomy 21, with a nuchal translucency of 3.6 mm and PAPP-A of 0.3 MoM. The foetus has a present nasal bone and no structural anomaly is visible on this morning's ultrasound. She has reviewed both contingent NIPT and definitive diagnostic options with her midwife and consultant and has decided that she wants a definitive answer in the shortest time possible because she has finite leave from work and significant anxiety. Her previous child has Down syndrome diagnosed postnatally and she does not want to wait until 15 weeks. The placenta is anterior and easily accessible by transabdominal route. She is RhD positive, has no allergies, and is fit and well. She asks the maternal foetal medicine consultant which diagnostic procedure is most appropriate today and what its principal procedure-related risk is.

data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, anterior accessible placenta on ultrasound today.
data_table.Investigations: cFTS adjusted T21 risk 1 in 60. NT 3.6 mm. PAPP-A 0.3 MoM. USS today: CRL consistent with 11+5, no structural anomalies, anterior placenta.

lead_in: Which diagnostic procedure is most appropriate today and what is the approximate procedure-related miscarriage risk in an experienced Australian unit?

options.text: Transcervical chorionic villus sampling today, with procedure-related miscarriage risk approximately 2 to 3%
options.rationale: Transcervical CVS has slightly higher infection and miscarriage rates than transabdominal in most series and is used when the placenta is posterior and inaccessible transabdominally. A figure of 2 to 3% overstates the risk in experienced units (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Amniocentesis today at 11+5 weeks, with procedure-related miscarriage risk approximately 0.5 to 1%
options.rationale: Amniocentesis before 15 weeks (so-called early amniocentesis) is no longer offered because of higher foetal loss and talipes equinovarus shown in the Canadian Early Amniocentesis Trial. CVS is the correct first-trimester option (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Defer diagnostic testing and arrange NIPT today, with procedure-related miscarriage risk of zero
options.rationale: NIPT is non-invasive but remains a screening test; she has already had a high-risk cFTS and explicitly wants a definitive answer. Offering only NIPT does not meet her stated need (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Transabdominal chorionic villus sampling today, with procedure-related miscarriage risk approximately 0.1 to 0.2%
options.rationale: CVS is performed from 10 to 13+6 weeks (most commonly 11 to 13 weeks); transabdominal CVS is preferred when the placenta is accessible. Australian and international meta-analyses estimate procedure-related miscarriage in experienced units at 0.1 to 0.2%, similar to amniocentesis (RANZCOG; HGSA).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing
HGSA/RANZCOG Joint statement on prenatal screening

options.text: Defer diagnostic testing until 18 to 20 weeks and proceed to morphology scan only, with procedure-related miscarriage risk of zero
options.rationale: A morphology scan is a screening tool, not diagnostic for aneuploidy, and does not satisfy her wish for early definitive information; up to 50% of T21 foetuses have a normal morphology scan (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

explanation.summary: CVS (10 to 13+6 weeks) and amniocentesis (from 15 weeks) are the two diagnostic options for foetal chromosomal analysis. Procedure-related miscarriage risk is approximately 0.1 to 0.2% in experienced units for both. Early amniocentesis (before 15 weeks) is contraindicated due to higher foetal loss and talipes.
key_points: CVS window: 10 to 13+6 weeks, transabdominal preferred if placenta accessible
Amniocentesis from 15 weeks; never before 15 due to Canadian Early Amnio Trial findings
Procedure-related miscarriage 0.1 to 0.2% for either in experienced units
Anti-D 625 IU IM after any procedure in an RhD-negative woman
QF-PCR results in 24 to 48 hours for common aneuploidies; full karyotype 1 to 2 weeks; microarray 1 to 2 weeks
explanation.pearls: If the placenta is posterior and inaccessible at 11 to 13 weeks, wait two weeks for amniocentesis rather than attempting transcervical CVS routinely.

sources.label: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing
HGSA/RANZCOG Joint statement on prenatal screening

subtopic_detail: Morphology scan - soft marker (echogenic intracardiac focus)
stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 woman attends her general practitioner for review of her 19 week and 4 day morphology scan, performed at an accredited Australian foetal medicine imaging service. She had a low-risk combined first-trimester screen at 12+5 weeks (adjusted T21 risk 1 in 4200, NT 1.4 mm). The morphology scan report describes normal biometry on the 50th centile, a structurally normal foetus on the standard RANZCOG/ASUM views, a normally implanted posterior placenta clear of the os, normal amniotic fluid volume, and an isolated echogenic intracardiac focus in the left ventricle as the only abnormal finding. The cardiac four-chamber view, outflow tracts and three-vessel view are otherwise normal. There are no other soft markers (nuchal fold normal, no echogenic bowel, no pyelectasis, no short femur or humerus, no choroid plexus cyst). She has no significant medical history, no family history of congenital heart disease and is not on any medications. She is anxious because she has read online that EIF is associated with Down syndrome and asks her general practitioner what this finding means in her context and what she should do next.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, fundus at the umbilicus.
data_table.Investigations: cFTS at 12+5: T21 risk 1 in 4200, NT 1.4 mm. Morphology scan 19+4: biometry 50th centile, structurally normal, isolated left ventricular EIF, no other soft markers, normal placenta and fluid.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate counselling and management plan?

options.text: Offer additional non-invasive prenatal testing today to further confirm low aneuploidy risk before reassuring her about the isolated EIF finding
options.rationale: NIPT can be offered if the woman wishes, but it is not indicated by an isolated EIF in a woman who already has a low-risk cFTS; the residual probability of aneuploidy is too low for routine contingent screening to be cost-effective (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Refer urgently to maternal foetal medicine and offer amniocentesis given the increased risk of trisomy 21 from the isolated EIF
options.rationale: Isolated EIF in a low-risk population carries a small likelihood ratio (about 1.4 to 1.8) that does not meaningfully change a prior risk of 1 in 4200; offering amniocentesis exposes the woman to a procedure-related miscarriage risk that exceeds the residual aneuploidy probability (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Arrange a foetal echocardiogram urgently to exclude an associated structural cardiac anomaly given the EIF finding
options.rationale: An isolated EIF in the absence of other findings on a high-quality four-chamber, outflow tract and three-vessel view is not an indication for foetal echocardiography; foetal echo is indicated for diabetes, NT >=3.5 mm, family history of CHD, or other suspicious findings (RANZCOG; ASUM).

options.text: Repeat the morphology scan in four weeks to confirm persistence of the EIF before deciding on further investigations
options.rationale: An isolated EIF is a static finding requiring no surveillance; repeat scanning is not recommended unless there are growth or other concerns (ASUM).

options.text: Isolated echogenic intracardiac focus in this low-risk woman does not meaningfully alter her aneuploidy risk; reassure and continue routine antenatal care
options.rationale: An isolated EIF in a woman with a low-risk first-trimester screen and no other soft markers does not meaningfully alter the prior risk of aneuploidy and is not an indication for diagnostic testing. RANZCOG and ASUM advise reassurance in this setting; the focus is most often a benign mineralisation in a papillary muscle (RANZCOG; ASUM).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

explanation.summary: Isolated soft markers such as an echogenic intracardiac focus, choroid plexus cyst or mild pyelectasis carry only modest likelihood ratios and should be interpreted in the context of the prior screening risk. In a woman with a low-risk cFTS and no other soft markers, isolated EIF does not change management.
key_points: Soft markers: nuchal fold >=6 mm, echogenic bowel, short humerus/femur, EIF, pyelectasis >=4 mm, choroid plexus cyst
Isolated EIF likelihood ratio for T21 ~1.4 to 1.8
Nuchal fold >=6 mm has highest likelihood ratio (LR ~17 to 20)
Foetal echo if NT >=3.5 mm, diabetes, family history CHD, or structural cardiac concern
Soft markers do not require repeat scanning if isolated
explanation.pearls: Always reframe soft markers using the woman's pre-test risk - the absolute residual aneuploidy probability matters more than the likelihood ratio in isolation.

sources.label: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

subtopic_detail: Anti-D prophylaxis - sensitising event before 12 weeks
stem: A 24-year-old G2P0 woman presents to a regional Australian emergency department with a single episode of moderate per-vaginal bleeding requiring two pads in two hours, associated with mild lower abdominal cramping. Her last menstrual period dates her at 9 weeks and 1 day, and a transvaginal ultrasound today shows a single intrauterine pregnancy with CRL consistent with 8+6 weeks, present foetal cardiac activity at 158 bpm, no retained products in the uterus and a small subchorionic haematoma. There is no cervical motion tenderness, the os is closed and no products of conception are visible. Her booking blood group from the GP last week is documented as O RhD negative with a negative antibody screen, and she had no prior pregnancies carried beyond miscarriage at 6 weeks one year ago. The general practice doctor in ED asks the obstetric registrar whether routine anti-D Rh(D) immunoglobulin is indicated for this episode and at what dose, given Australian National Blood Authority guidance.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.7 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, mild suprapubic tenderness. Speculum: small amount of fresh blood, os closed, no products. No CMT.
data_table.Investigations: Blood group O RhD negative, antibody screen negative. TV USS: viable IUP at 8+6, FHR 158 bpm, small subchorionic haematoma, no retained products. Beta-hCG 28 000 IU/L.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate anti-D Rh(D) immunoglobulin management today?

options.text: Administer 625 IU anti-D Rh(D) immunoglobulin IM now, since any bleeding in an RhD-negative woman after 9 weeks is a sensitising event
options.rationale: The Australian NBA guideline states that anti-D is not routinely indicated for a sensitising event before 12 completed weeks, except for chorionic villus sampling, ectopic pregnancy, molar pregnancy or termination of pregnancy (NBA Anti-D guidelines).
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

options.text: Administer 1500 IU anti-D Rh(D) immunoglobulin IM now as a single antenatal dose covering both 28 week and 34 week prophylaxis until delivery
options.rationale: 1500 IU is the UK single-dose antenatal regimen at 28 weeks; Australia uses two-dose 625 IU IM at 28 and 34 weeks, not a single 1500 IU dose (NBA).
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

options.text: Administer 250 IU anti-D Rh(D) immunoglobulin IM now and order a Kleihauer-Betke test today to titrate any further required dose
options.rationale: 250 IU is the UK dose; the Australian dose for first-trimester sensitising events (when indicated, e.g. after CVS) is 250 IU pre-12 weeks per NBA. Kleihauer testing is recommended for events from 20 weeks, not first-trimester bleeding (NBA).
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

options.text: Anti-D is not indicated today; she continues routine 28 and 34 week prophylaxis of 625 IU IM with 625 IU post-event from 12 weeks onwards
options.rationale: Per the National Blood Authority guidelines, sensitising events before 12 completed weeks (threatened or complete miscarriage with no instrumentation, minor uterine bleeding) do not require anti-D. Routine antenatal anti-D 625 IU IM is given at 28 and 34 weeks, and 625 IU IM is given for any sensitising event from 12 weeks onwards (with Kleihauer-Betke if >20 weeks to quantify additional dose) (NBA).
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

options.text: Defer all anti-D decisions until 12 weeks gestation, then administer 625 IU IM only if vaginal bleeding persists or recurs at that time
options.rationale: Anti-D is given for sensitising events when they occur, not prophylactically deferred; routine antenatal prophylaxis begins at 28 weeks, not 12. The first-trimester decision is whether the event qualifies, not when to delay (NBA).
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

explanation.summary: Australian anti-D guidance (NBA) does not recommend anti-D for sensitising events before 12 completed weeks except CVS, ectopic, molar pregnancy or termination. From 12 weeks, give 625 IU IM after a sensitising event within 72 hours. Routine antenatal prophylaxis is 625 IU IM at both 28 and 34 weeks, and 625 IU IM after birth if the baby is RhD positive (Kleihauer if >20 weeks event to titrate).
key_points: Pre-12 week sensitising events: anti-D only for CVS, ectopic, molar, ToP
12 to 20 weeks: 625 IU IM after sensitising event (within 72 hours)
>20 weeks: 625 IU IM plus Kleihauer-Betke to quantify additional dose
Routine antenatal: 625 IU IM at 28 AND 34 weeks (two-dose Australian regimen)
Postnatal: 625 IU IM if baby RhD positive within 72 hours
explanation.pearls: Australian 625 IU IM is not interchangeable with the UK 1500 IU or 250 IU regimens - always cite the NBA guideline in Australian practice.

sources.label: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

subtopic_detail: GBS screening - intrapartum prophylaxis with penicillin allergy
stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman presents to the birth suite at 38 weeks and 5 days gestation in established labour with regular painful contractions every 3 minutes and intact membranes. Her vaginal-rectal swab taken at 36 weeks for universal group B Streptococcus screening was positive for Streptococcus agalactiae, sensitive to penicillin and reported as resistant to clindamycin and erythromycin. Her chart documents an immediate hypersensitivity reaction (generalised urticaria and tongue swelling without anaphylaxis) following oral amoxicillin in childhood, judged by the allergy team as high-risk for severe penicillin allergy. Her previous baby is well at age 3 with no neonatal sepsis. Vital signs are normal, the baby is cephalic and engaged, foetal heart rate is reassuring on intermittent auscultation, and the obstetric registrar wants to commence intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis. The midwife asks which antibiotic regimen is most appropriate given the patient's penicillin allergy and the documented GBS antibiotic sensitivities.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/74 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Cervix 5 cm dilated, fully effaced, vertex at -1 station. Contractions 3 in 10 strong.
data_table.Investigations: GBS swab at 36 weeks positive, sensitive to penicillin, resistant to clindamycin and erythromycin. FBC normal. Group O RhD positive.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis regimen?

options.text: IV benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose then 1.8 g every 4 hours until birth as standard first-line intrapartum prophylaxis
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin is the first-line agent in non-allergic women but is contraindicated by her documented immediate hypersensitivity reaction (RANZCOG GBS; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: IV cefazolin 2 g loading dose then 1 g every 8 hours until birth as the recommended alternative for low-risk penicillin allergy
options.rationale: Cefazolin is the alternative in women with low-risk penicillin allergy (non-anaphylactic, non-immediate). Her immediate hypersensitivity reaction with mucosal involvement is high-risk and cephalosporins are not recommended (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: IV vancomycin 25 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) loading then 15 mg/kg every 12 hours until birth
options.rationale: For high-risk penicillin allergy with GBS resistant to clindamycin and erythromycin, IV vancomycin is the recommended intrapartum prophylaxis in Australia. Dosing is weight-based loading then maintenance, with at least 4 hours before birth ideally for adequate cord blood levels (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: IV clindamycin 900 mg every 8 hours until birth, given the documented high-risk penicillin allergy and inability to use beta-lactams
options.rationale: Clindamycin is an option in high-risk penicillin allergy only when GBS sensitivity to clindamycin is confirmed; her isolate is resistant, making this inappropriate (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Oral erythromycin 500 mg every 6 hours until birth, given documented penicillin allergy and need to avoid intravenous antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: Oral therapy does not achieve adequate cord blood levels for intrapartum prophylaxis, and her GBS isolate is resistant to erythromycin (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: Australia uses universal vaginal-rectal GBS screening at 35 to 37 weeks. Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis is given to GBS-positive women, prior GBS-affected baby, GBS bacteriuria in this pregnancy, intrapartum fever, preterm labour <37 weeks with unknown status, or ROM >=18 hours with unknown status.
key_points: First line: IV benzylpenicillin 3 g load then 1.8 g every 4 hours (or ampicillin 2 g load then 1 g every 4 hours)
Low-risk penicillin allergy: IV cefazolin 2 g load then 1 g every 8 hours
High-risk penicillin allergy with clindamycin-sensitive GBS: IV clindamycin 900 mg every 8 hours
High-risk penicillin allergy with clindamycin-resistant GBS: IV vancomycin
Aim for >=4 hours of antibiotic before birth for adequate cord levels
explanation.pearls: Always check the GBS isolate sensitivities at the time of the positive swab - inducible clindamycin resistance is common and is the reason vancomycin is the safe fallback.

stem: A 31-year-old G1P0 woman attends her general practice for review of her 75 g oral glucose tolerance test performed at 26 weeks gestation as routine ADIPS-recommended screening. She has no prior diabetes and a body mass index of 28 kg/m2. Her parents both have type 2 diabetes on metformin. She has had no symptoms of polyuria, polydipsia or weight loss. Her morphology scan at 19 weeks was normal, with foetal biometry on the 60th centile. Her booking bloods were unremarkable. The OGTT was performed after an overnight 10-hour fast at a local pathology collection centre with serial venous plasma glucose samples at 0, 60 and 120 minutes after the 75 g glucose load. Her fasting venous plasma glucose was 5.3 mmol/L, the 1-hour value was 9.6 mmol/L and the 2-hour value was 7.9 mmol/L. She has no acute symptoms, blood pressure is 118/72 mmHg and the foetal heart rate today is 142 bpm on bedside auscultation. She asks the general practitioner whether the test indicates gestational diabetes by Australian (ADIPS) criteria.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, BMI 28 kg/m2.
data_table.Examination: Fundus 26 cm. Foetal heart 142 bpm. No oedema.
data_table.Investigations: 75 g OGTT at 26 weeks: fasting 5.3 mmol/L, 1-hour 9.6 mmol/L, 2-hour 7.9 mmol/L. HbA1c 5.5%.

lead_in: Which best describes the interpretation of this OGTT result against current ADIPS criteria?

options.text: Gestational diabetes is diagnosed because the fasting glucose of 5.3 mmol/L exceeds the ADIPS fasting threshold of 5.1 mmol/L
options.rationale: Current ADIPS diagnostic thresholds for the 75 g OGTT at 24 to 28 weeks are fasting >=5.1 mmol/L, 1-hour >=10.0 mmol/L, or 2-hour >=8.5 mmol/L; any one threshold met or exceeded is diagnostic. Her fasting value of 5.3 meets the fasting criterion (ADIPS).

options.text: Gestational diabetes is not diagnosed because no single value reaches the WHO threshold of 7.0 mmol/L fasting or 11.1 mmol/L at 2 hours
options.rationale: WHO 7.0/11.1 thresholds are for overt diabetes diagnosis outside pregnancy; Australian ADIPS thresholds for GDM are 5.1/10.0/8.5 (ADIPS).

options.text: Gestational diabetes is not diagnosed because at least two values must meet thresholds under ADIPS criteria
options.rationale: ADIPS adopts the IADPSG single-value rule: any single value at or above threshold is diagnostic; two abnormal values are not required (ADIPS).

options.text: Gestational diabetes is not diagnosed because the 1-hour value of 9.6 mmol/L is below the 10.0 mmol/L threshold; the fasting value of 5.3 is within the normal pregnancy range
options.rationale: 5.3 mmol/L exceeds the ADIPS fasting threshold of 5.1; meeting any one threshold is diagnostic of GDM regardless of the other two values (ADIPS).

options.text: The diagnosis should be deferred and HbA1c used to confirm; HbA1c of 5.5% rules out gestational diabetes
options.rationale: HbA1c is not used as the primary diagnostic test for GDM (it lacks sensitivity in the second trimester); the OGTT remains the standard, and a single threshold value is diagnostic (ADIPS).

explanation.summary: Australia uses ADIPS thresholds for the 75 g OGTT at 24 to 28 weeks: fasting >=5.1, 1-hour >=10.0, 2-hour >=8.5 mmol/L. Any one value meeting threshold diagnoses GDM. Early OGTT at booking is offered to women with risk factors (prior GDM, BMI >=30, previous macrosomic baby, family history, certain ethnicities).
key_points: ADIPS OGTT thresholds: fasting >=5.1, 1-hour >=10.0, 2-hour >=8.5 mmol/L
Any single value meeting threshold = GDM
Standard screening at 24 to 28 weeks; early OGTT at booking for high-risk women
Management ladder: diet/exercise, then metformin and/or insulin; insulin is first-line agent if pharmacotherapy needed in many AU centres
Postpartum 75 g OGTT at 6 to 12 weeks to detect persistent diabetes; lifelong screening every 1 to 3 years
explanation.pearls: Memorise 5.1 / 10.0 / 8.5 for ADIPS; do not use older Australian thresholds (e.g. ADIPS 1998 5.5/8.0) or international cut-offs.

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman attends an early pregnancy assessment service with a referral from her general practitioner. She is at 10 weeks and 3 days gestation by certain last menstrual period, confirmed by a dating ultrasound at 7 weeks that showed CRL of 9 mm with present foetal cardiac activity. She has had minimal brown vaginal spotting for the last week but no heavy bleeding or pain, and has noticed that breast tenderness and nausea (previously prominent) have resolved over the past 10 days. She has no fever, no haemodynamic instability and no significant past medical history. Examination shows a soft, non-tender abdomen with a closed cervical os, no active bleeding and no products of conception visible. The transvaginal ultrasound today shows a single intrauterine gestational sac with mean sac diameter 28 mm, a foetal pole with crown-rump length of 12 mm and no foetal cardiac activity demonstrated on careful M-mode assessment by an accredited sonographer. There is no retained tissue, no subchorionic haematoma and the cervix is closed.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen. Speculum: minimal brown discharge, os closed, no products. No CMT.
data_table.Investigations: TV USS: single IUP, MSD 28 mm, CRL 12 mm, no foetal cardiac activity on M-mode. Cervix closed. No retained tissue.

lead_in: Which classification best describes this clinical and ultrasound picture?

options.text: Threatened miscarriage - viable intrauterine pregnancy with bleeding and closed cervix
options.rationale: Threatened miscarriage requires confirmation of foetal viability; the foetal pole has CRL 12 mm with no cardiac activity, which is diagnostic of foetal demise (RANZCOG; ASUM).

options.text: Missed miscarriage - foetal demise diagnosed on ultrasound with retained pregnancy tissue and a closed cervical os
options.rationale: Missed (silent) miscarriage is defined as ultrasound confirmation of foetal demise (CRL >=7 mm with no cardiac activity, or MSD >=25 mm with no foetal pole on transvaginal scan) with retained pregnancy tissue and a closed cervix. Her CRL of 12 mm with no cardiac activity meets the diagnostic ASUM/RCOG criteria (RANZCOG; ASUM).

options.text: Inevitable miscarriage - bleeding with open cervix and foetal demise on ultrasound
options.rationale: Inevitable miscarriage requires an open cervical os; her os is closed (RANZCOG).

options.text: Incomplete miscarriage - retained products of conception following partial expulsion of pregnancy tissue
options.rationale: Incomplete miscarriage requires that pregnancy tissue has been partially passed, with retained products on ultrasound; she has had no significant bleeding and the pregnancy tissue is intact (RANZCOG).

options.text: Complete miscarriage - all products of conception passed with an empty uterus and resolving symptoms
options.rationale: Complete miscarriage requires an empty uterus on ultrasound following passage of all products; her uterus contains the foetal pole and gestational sac (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Miscarriage classification is based on clinical features, cervical os status and ultrasound findings. Missed miscarriage is foetal demise with retained tissue and closed os, often with resolution of pregnancy symptoms. Diagnostic ultrasound criteria are CRL >=7 mm with no cardiac activity OR MSD >=25 mm with no foetal pole on TV scan.
key_points: Threatened: viable IUP, bleeding, closed os
Inevitable: open os, bleeding, often POC at os
Incomplete: partial passage, retained POC on USS
Complete: empty uterus on USS, resolving symptoms/hCG
Missed: foetal demise, closed os, retained tissue
Diagnostic USS: CRL >=7 mm + no FH, or MSD >=25 mm + no foetal pole on TV
explanation.pearls: Always confirm foetal demise with a second opinion or repeat scan 7 to 14 days later if the CRL is borderline (close to 7 mm) before offering definitive management.

stem: A 29-year-old G2P0 woman is reviewed in a tertiary early pregnancy assessment unit at 6 weeks and 4 days gestation by last menstrual period after referral from her general practitioner with mild right iliac fossa pain and intermittent light per-vaginal spotting for 48 hours. Her previous pregnancy was a first-trimester miscarriage; she has no history of pelvic infection, prior ectopic, tubal surgery, IUD or assisted reproduction. She is haemodynamically stable, afebrile, and has no shoulder-tip pain, dizziness or peritonism. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus with no intrauterine gestational sac, no free fluid in the pouch of Douglas, and an unruptured right adnexal mass measuring 28 mm containing a yolk sac but no foetal pole or cardiac activity. There is no haemoperitoneum. Her beta-hCG today is 3800 IU/L (yesterday 3500 IU/L), liver function tests, full blood count and renal function are normal, and she has no contraindications to methotrexate including no breastfeeding, no peptic ulcer disease, no significant pulmonary or hepatic disease. She lives 20 minutes from the hospital, has reliable transport, can attend for follow-up blood tests, and is keen to avoid surgery if reasonable. The obstetric registrar is considering single-dose intramuscular methotrexate as outpatient management and is checking each eligibility criterion against the RANZCOG and RCOG protocols.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, T 36.7 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Mild RIF tenderness, no peritonism. Soft abdomen. No CMT. No active bleeding.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 3800 IU/L (yesterday 3500). FBC, UEC, LFTs normal. TV USS: empty uterus, no free fluid, right adnexal mass 28 mm with yolk sac, no foetal cardiac activity.

lead_in: Which feature in this presentation, if changed, would most clearly contraindicate single-dose IM methotrexate as outpatient management?

options.text: An adnexal mass measuring 28 mm with a yolk sac present but no foetal cardiac activity
options.rationale: The RANZCOG/RCOG eligibility criteria include adnexal mass <35 mm and absence of foetal cardiac activity; her mass is within this size and there is no cardiac activity. This finding does not contraindicate methotrexate (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: A serum beta-hCG of 3800 IU/L with a 24-hour rise of 300 IU/L
options.rationale: Methotrexate eligibility usually requires beta-hCG <5000 IU/L (some units 1500 to 5000); 3800 is below threshold and a small rise is consistent with ectopic. This is within the eligible range (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Foetal cardiac activity demonstrated on the transvaginal ultrasound today within the right adnexal mass
options.rationale: The presence of foetal cardiac activity within the ectopic is an explicit contraindication to single-dose methotrexate in both RANZCOG and RCOG guidance because failure rates are much higher; salpingectomy or salpingostomy is the recommended management. Other absolute contraindications are haemodynamic instability, ruptured ectopic, significant haemoperitoneum, intrauterine pregnancy, breastfeeding, immunodeficiency, active hepatic or renal disease, blood dyscrasia, and inability to comply with follow-up (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Mild right iliac fossa tenderness without peritonism, fever or haemodynamic instability
options.rationale: Mild pain in a stable patient with no peritonism is not a contraindication and is common in tubal ectopic; only acute severe pain or peritonism would mandate surgery (RANZCOG).

options.text: Residence 20 minutes from the hospital with reliable transport and stated willingness to attend follow-up
options.rationale: Good access to care and willingness to follow up are pre-requisites for outpatient methotrexate and support, rather than contraindicate, this management option (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Single-dose IM methotrexate (typically 50 mg/m2 in Australian units) is suitable for haemodynamically stable women with an unruptured tubal ectopic of <35 mm, no cardiac activity, beta-hCG <5000 IU/L, no contraindications and reliable follow-up. Failure or rising hCG triggers a second dose or surgery.
key_points: Methotrexate dose: 50 mg/m2 IM single dose, repeat at day 7 if <15% fall day 4 to 7
Eligibility: stable, unruptured, mass <35 mm, no FCA, hCG <5000, normal LFTs/FBC/UEC, no breastfeeding
Absolute contraindications: ruptured ectopic, haemodynamic instability, FCA, IUP coexists, blood dyscrasia, hepatic/renal disease, immunodeficiency, peptic ulcer disease, breastfeeding
Avoid pregnancy for 3 months after methotrexate; folate supplementation pre-conception
Surgery (laparoscopic salpingectomy) if unstable, ruptured, large mass, FCA, or contraindications to MTX
explanation.pearls: Always confirm reliable follow-up before MTX - missed day 4 and day 7 hCG checks is the commonest reason for treatment failure leading to rupture.

subtopic_detail: Pregnancy of unknown location - serial beta-hCG and discriminatory zone
stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 woman presents to an early pregnancy assessment unit with a positive urine pregnancy test, light per-vaginal spotting for 3 days and intermittent mild lower abdominal cramping. By last menstrual period she is approximately 6 weeks gestation but her cycles have been irregular over the last 12 months while she has been training for a half-marathon. She is haemodynamically stable, with no peritonism, shoulder-tip pain or dizziness. A high-quality transvaginal ultrasound performed by an experienced sonographer today shows an empty uterus with a thin endometrium, no intrauterine gestational sac, no adnexal mass, no free fluid in the pouch of Douglas and normal-appearing ovaries bilaterally. Her serum beta-hCG today is 1200 IU/L. She has no significant past medical or surgical history, no risk factors for ectopic pregnancy (no prior pelvic infection, IUD, tubal surgery or assisted reproductive technology), and no contraindications to methotrexate. She is well, has reliable transport and is willing to attend for repeat blood tests. The clinic registrar must decide whether to expectantly manage with serial hCG and rescanning above the discriminatory zone, image again immediately, treat empirically as ectopic with methotrexate, or reassure as presumed early viable intrauterine pregnancy below the imaging threshold.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, no peritonism, no CMT. Os closed, minimal brown spotting.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 1200 IU/L today. TV USS: empty uterus, thin endometrium, no adnexal mass, no free fluid, normal ovaries.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence single-dose IM methotrexate 50 mg/m2 today as presumed ectopic pregnancy based on the empty uterus on transvaginal ultrasound
options.rationale: A pregnancy of unknown location with hCG below the transvaginal discriminatory zone (approximately 1500 to 2000 IU/L) and no ectopic seen on ultrasound is not yet diagnosed as ectopic; treating with methotrexate could terminate a viable early intrauterine pregnancy (RANZCOG).

options.text: Arrange diagnostic laparoscopy today for definitive diagnosis and possible salpingectomy because of the empty uterus and positive pregnancy test
options.rationale: Diagnostic laparoscopy is reserved for haemodynamic instability, suspected rupture or persistent diagnostic uncertainty after failed expectant management; it is not first line for PUL in a stable woman (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat the serum beta-hCG in 48 hours and rescan once it exceeds the discriminatory zone of 1500 to 2000 IU/L, with clear safety-netting
options.rationale: Pregnancy of unknown location in a stable woman with hCG below the discriminatory zone is managed with serial 48-hour hCG and rescanning once the hCG exceeds the threshold. A normal early intrauterine pregnancy doubles hCG every 48 to 72 hours; a fall >=50% suggests failing pregnancy; a plateau or sub-optimal rise increases ectopic concern. Safety-netting and ready re-presentation are essential (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Repeat the transvaginal ultrasound today by a second sonographer to confirm absence of an intrauterine pregnancy before any follow-up
options.rationale: A high-quality scan has already been performed; immediate repeat scanning is low-yield. Waiting for the hCG to cross the discriminatory zone is the standard approach (RANZCOG).

options.text: Reassure that this is a normal early intrauterine pregnancy and arrange routine antenatal booking for 8 to 10 weeks
options.rationale: Reassurance without exclusion of ectopic risks rupture; PUL must be followed up to a definitive diagnosis (IUP, failing PUL, ectopic) (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Pregnancy of unknown location is defined as a positive pregnancy test with no intrauterine or extrauterine pregnancy on high-quality TV ultrasound. Management is risk-stratified by serial beta-hCG every 48 hours and rescanning once the hCG exceeds the discriminatory zone (approximately 1500 to 2000 IU/L on TV scan).
key_points: Discriminatory zone TV scan: 1500 to 2000 IU/L (some Australian units 1500)
Normal early IUP: hCG doubles every 48 to 72 hours
Failing PUL: >=50% fall in 48 hours
Suboptimal rise (<63% in 48 h) or plateau: high risk of ectopic
Safety net: PV bleeding, severe pain, shoulder-tip pain, dizziness - immediate review
explanation.pearls: Never treat a PUL with methotrexate based on a single hCG and an empty uterus; you must exclude a viable early IUP first via serial hCG and repeat imaging.

subtopic_detail: Hyperemesis - severity assessment and admission criteria
stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 woman at 10 weeks and 2 days gestation presents to an emergency department with three weeks of progressively worsening nausea and vomiting. Over the last 48 hours she has been unable to keep down oral fluids, has vomited at least eight times per day, and has lost 4 kg from her booking weight of 62 kg (>5%). She is exhausted, intermittently tearful, and has missed two days of work. Her partner reports she has had episodes of postural light-headedness. Examination shows dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 2 to 3 seconds, mild ketotic breath, HR 108 bpm, BP 100/62 mmHg lying and 88/55 mmHg standing, T 36.7 degC. Urinalysis shows ketones 3+, specific gravity 1.030, no nitrites or leucocytes. Her PUQE-24 score today is 14 out of 15 (severe). Bloods reveal sodium 132 mmol/L, potassium 3.1 mmol/L, urea 7.8 mmol/L, creatinine 92 micromol/L. Beta-hCG and TFTs are pending. A transabdominal scan shows a viable singleton pregnancy at 10+2 with no molar features. She has tried oral pyridoxine 25 mg TDS at home with no relief.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 100/62 lying, 88/55 standing, HR 108 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Dry mucosa, CR 2 to 3s, ketotic breath. Soft abdomen. Uterus not yet palpable.
data_table.Investigations: PUQE-24 14/15 (severe). Weight loss 4 kg (>5%). Urine ketones 3+, SG 1.030. Na 132, K 3.1, urea 7.8, creat 92. USS: viable singleton, no molar features.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management step?

options.text: Admit for IV normal saline rehydration, IV thiamine before any dextrose, IV potassium replacement, and stepwise antiemetics with serial electrolyte monitoring
options.rationale: She meets admission criteria for hyperemesis gravidarum: severe PUQE, >5% weight loss, dehydration with postural hypotension, ketonuria, hypokalaemia and failed oral therapy. Treatment is IV rehydration (commonly normal saline with KCl), thiamine 100 mg IV/IM daily before any dextrose to prevent Wernicke's, a stepwise antiemetic (pyridoxine-doxylamine, metoclopramide or prochlorperazine, ondansetron), and serial electrolyte and weight monitoring (RANZCOG; SOMANZ Hyperemesis).
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy

options.text: Discharge with oral doxylamine-pyridoxine and a metoclopramide PRN script with a written safety-net to return for ongoing vomiting
options.rationale: Severe PUQE, postural hypotension, hypokalaemia, ketonuria and >5% weight loss are explicit indications for admission and IV rehydration, not outpatient antiemetics (RANZCOG; SOMANZ Hyperemesis).
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy

options.text: Admit for total parenteral nutrition via a central venous catheter to bypass the gut entirely while severe vomiting resolves over coming days
options.rationale: TPN is reserved for women who fail standard rehydration and antiemetic regimens with persistent weight loss; it is not first-line and carries significant infection/thromboembolism risk (RANZCOG; SOMANZ).

options.text: Admit for IV dextrose 5% rehydration to provide immediate energy substrate and commence IV ondansetron monotherapy without giving prior thiamine
options.rationale: Dextrose-first rehydration without thiamine cover risks Wernicke's encephalopathy in hyperemesis. Use normal saline; give thiamine before any dextrose (RANZCOG; SOMANZ).
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy

options.text: Arrange urgent termination of pregnancy at 10 weeks as the only definitive treatment for refractory severe hyperemesis unresponsive to oral therapy
options.rationale: Termination is rarely indicated and only after exhausting medical management with the woman's informed wish; the great majority of hyperemesis resolves by 16 to 20 weeks (RANZCOG; SOMANZ).

explanation.summary: Admission criteria for hyperemesis: severe PUQE-24, >5% weight loss, persistent vomiting despite oral antiemetics, ketonuria, dehydration with postural hypotension or tachycardia, electrolyte derangement (hypokalaemia, hyponatraemia), or inability to tolerate oral therapy. Treatment is IV rehydration with thiamine before dextrose, antiemetic ladder and electrolyte correction.
key_points: PUQE-24: 12+ = severe
Antiemetic ladder: pyridoxine-doxylamine -> metoclopramide / prochlorperazine -> ondansetron -> corticosteroids (refractory)
Thiamine 100 mg IV/IM daily BEFORE any dextrose (Wernicke prevention)
VTE prophylaxis on admission
Investigate for molar pregnancy, multiple pregnancy, thyroid storm if atypical features
explanation.pearls: Always check TFTs and exclude molar/multiple pregnancy in severe hyperemesis; suppressed TSH from beta-hCG cross-reactivity is common and rarely requires treatment.

sources.label: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy

stem: A 22-year-old G1P0 woman presents to her general practitioner at an estimated 11 weeks gestation by uncertain last menstrual period with three weeks of persistent nausea, intermittent vaginal spotting and one episode of passing what she describes as small grape-like tissue. She has no significant medical history. Examination shows a uterus palpable above the symphysis to a size consistent with 14 weeks, soft and non-tender. Blood pressure today is 138/86 mmHg (previously 110/70 at GP visit at 8 weeks). She has dry mucous membranes and reports mild postural symptoms. A transvaginal ultrasound at the local imaging service today shows a heterogeneous intrauterine mass with multiple small cystic spaces (described as 'snowstorm' appearance), no foetal pole, no foetal cardiac activity, and bilateral theca lutein cysts on the ovaries measuring 4 to 5 cm. The serum beta-hCG is 285 000 IU/L. Thyroid function tests show TSH 0.05 mU/L and free T4 22 pmol/L (mildly elevated). She has not been on any thyroid medication. She has been referred to the local gynaecology team and her GP wants to confirm the recognition and what the next steps are.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/86 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Uterus size at 14 weeks (LMP-dated 11 weeks). Dry mucosa. Mild postural light-headedness.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 285 000 IU/L. TSH 0.05 mU/L, fT4 22 pmol/L. TV USS: heterogeneous 'snowstorm' mass, no foetal pole, bilateral theca lutein cysts 4 to 5 cm.

lead_in: Which sequence of management most accurately reflects current Australian (RANZCOG) guidance for this presentation?

options.text: Single-dose IM methotrexate 50 mg/m2 as outpatient with weekly hCG until undetectable, then 6-monthly surveillance for the following 12 months
options.rationale: Methotrexate is first-line for persistent gestational trophoblastic neoplasia, not for primary evacuation of a hydatidiform mole. Surgical evacuation is the primary treatment (RANZCOG GTD).

options.text: Suction curettage in theatre with histology, weekly hCG until three negative samples then monthly for 6 months (complete) or 1 month (partial), with reliable contraception
options.rationale: RANZCOG and the Australian/New Zealand Gestational Trophoblastic Disease group recommend suction curettage as the primary treatment for hydatidiform mole. Histopathology confirms complete (no foetus, diploid androgenetic) vs partial (foetus or foetal parts, triploid) mole. Surveillance: weekly beta-hCG until three consecutive negative weekly samples, then monthly for 6 months (complete mole) or until one month after normal hCG (partial). Reliable contraception is mandatory throughout because pregnancy obscures rising hCG that would indicate persistent GTN (RANZCOG GTD).

options.text: Expectant management with weekly serum beta-hCG only, given that many molar pregnancies will resolve spontaneously without surgical or medical intervention
options.rationale: Molar pregnancies do not reliably resolve spontaneously; surgical evacuation with histological confirmation is required to confirm the diagnosis and remove the trophoblastic tissue (RANZCOG GTD).

options.text: Total abdominal hysterectomy as primary treatment given the high reported risk of choriocarcinoma associated with complete hydatidiform mole on histology
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is reserved for women who have completed their family AND have a complete mole, or for refractory disease; suction curettage is the standard first-line for women wishing to preserve fertility (RANZCOG GTD).

options.text: Medical evacuation of the mole with methylergometrine and misoprostol, followed by weekly serum beta-hCG until undetectable for three consecutive weekly samples
options.rationale: Medical evacuation of a mole with uterotonics or prostaglandins is associated with higher rates of incomplete evacuation, embolisation of trophoblastic tissue and persistent GTN. Suction curettage is preferred (RANZCOG GTD).

explanation.summary: Hydatidiform mole is treated by suction curettage in theatre with histopathological confirmation. Beta-hCG surveillance follows: weekly until three consecutive negative samples, then monthly for 6 months (complete) or one month (partial). Reliable contraception is required throughout because pregnancy obscures the rising hCG signal of persistent GTN.
key_points: Complete mole: no foetus, 'snowstorm' USS, hCG often >100 000, 15 to 20% progress to GTN
Partial mole: foetus/foetal parts, triploid, 1 to 5% progress to GTN
Primary management: suction curettage in theatre with histology
Surveillance: weekly hCG until 3 consecutive negative weekly samples, then monthly 6 months (complete) or 1 month (partial)
Contraception throughout surveillance; avoid pregnancy until cleared
Refer to GTD multidisciplinary service if hCG plateaus, rises, or new metastatic features develop
explanation.pearls: Suspect mole in any first-trimester pregnancy with hyperemesis, early pre-eclampsia, hyperthyroidism, very high hCG, or uterus large for dates - get a TV scan.

stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 woman is reviewed at her 12 week antenatal booking appointment. She is asymptomatic, has no urinary frequency, dysuria, suprapubic discomfort or fever, and has no significant past medical or surgical history. Her booking bloods are unremarkable. A bedside dipstick urinalysis on the midstream urine sample today is negative for nitrites, leucocytes, blood and protein. However, the laboratory midstream urine culture sent at the booking visit one week ago has come back this morning with pure growth of 10^8 colony-forming units per litre of Escherichia coli, fully sensitive to amoxicillin, cefalexin, nitrofurantoin and trimethoprim. She has no known drug allergies and is on no medications other than a routine pregnancy multivitamin. Vital signs are normal and there is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Her general practitioner asks the obstetric registrar by telephone whether this asymptomatic finding warrants antibiotic treatment given that her dipstick was clear and she has no symptoms.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, no costovertebral angle tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: MSU MCS: pure growth E. coli 10^8 CFU/L, sensitive to amoxicillin, cefalexin, nitrofurantoin, trimethoprim. Dipstick today negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: No antibiotic treatment is indicated because the patient is asymptomatic and the dipstick today is negative; repeat MSU MCS in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy is associated with a 20 to 30% risk of progression to pyelonephritis and increased risk of preterm birth and low birthweight; treatment is therefore recommended regardless of symptoms (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Commence empirical oral cefalexin 500 mg every 6 hours for 5 days, followed by a test of cure MSU one week after completion
options.rationale: Asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy is treated with a short course of an agent active against the cultured isolate, with a follow-up MSU after treatment to confirm clearance. Cefalexin is a safe first-line option in pregnancy per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; alternatives include nitrofurantoin (avoid at term due to neonatal haemolysis risk), amoxicillin and trimethoprim (avoid first trimester) (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Admit for IV gentamicin and amoxicillin treatment given the high colony count regardless of symptoms
options.rationale: IV antibiotics are reserved for pyelonephritis or sepsis; asymptomatic bacteriuria is treated with oral antibiotics in the community (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Treat only if a repeat MSU MCS in 1 week confirms persistent significant bacteriuria, given the possibility of contamination
options.rationale: A pure growth at 10^8 CFU/L is well above the diagnostic threshold for significant bacteriuria; repeating only delays treatment and increases the risk of pyelonephritis (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Treat with nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for 5 days and continue suppressive nitrofurantoin until delivery
options.rationale: Suppressive therapy throughout pregnancy is not standard for a first episode of asymptomatic bacteriuria; nitrofurantoin should also be avoided at term (>36 weeks) because of neonatal haemolysis risk (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: Asymptomatic bacteriuria affects 2 to 10% of pregnancies and carries a 20 to 30% risk of progression to pyelonephritis if untreated, plus an increased risk of preterm birth and low birthweight. Treat with a short course of a culture-directed agent and confirm clearance with a test-of-cure MSU.
key_points: Significant bacteriuria: single MSU with pure growth >=10^8 CFU/L
Dipstick has poor sensitivity (~50%); MSU MCS at booking is the standard
First-line agents (AU): cefalexin, nitrofurantoin (not at term), amoxicillin
Avoid trimethoprim in first trimester (folate antagonist); avoid nitrofurantoin at term
Test of cure 1 week post-treatment; recurrence -> investigate and consider suppression
explanation.pearls: Always check the booking MSU result actively at the 12 to 16 week visit - it is the commonest treatable cause of preterm birth and pyelonephritis that gets missed by dipstick.

subtopic_detail: Heterotopic pregnancy after ART - discrimination from PUL/ectopic
stem: A 34-year-old G3P0 woman conceived through a single fresh embryo transfer IVF cycle two weeks ago and presents to a tertiary early pregnancy assessment service at 6 weeks and 5 days gestation by transfer date. She has had two previous tubal ectopic pregnancies managed with laparoscopic salpingectomy (left partial and right partial), with severe periadhesive disease documented at the second operation, and an extensive endometriosis history with bilateral chocolate cysts treated 4 years ago. She takes only luteal-phase progesterone pessaries 200 mg twice daily as part of her IVF protocol and a pregnancy multivitamin; she has no other medications and no drug allergies. Over the last 72 hours she has developed unilateral left iliac fossa pain that is intermittent but increasing, with one episode of dizziness this morning when standing. She has had no vaginal bleeding and is haemodynamically stable on arrival. Vital signs: BP 108/68 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 36.6 degC, SpO2 99%, respiratory rate 16. Abdominal examination reveals tenderness in the left iliac fossa without peritonism; the cervical os is closed and there is no active bleeding on speculum. Serum beta-hCG today is 12 000 IU/L. Transvaginal ultrasound by an experienced foetal medicine sonographer demonstrates a single intrauterine gestational sac containing a yolk sac and a viable foetal pole with CRL 7 mm and foetal cardiac activity at 122 bpm. There is a separate left adnexal complex mass measuring 30 mm with a hyperechoic ring (sometimes called the 'tubal ring' or 'bagel sign') and a small amount of free fluid in the pouch of Douglas (approximately 50 mL). Her LFTs, FBC, renal function and group/screen are normal; she is RhD positive. The on-call gynaecology consultant has been called and is en route.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/68 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Left iliac fossa tenderness, no peritonism. Os closed. No active bleeding.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 12 000 IU/L. TV USS: viable intrauterine pregnancy at 6+5 (CRL 7 mm, FH 122 bpm) AND separate left adnexal complex mass 30 mm with echogenic ring; small free fluid POD ~50 mL.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Single-dose IM methotrexate 50 mg/m2 to treat the left adnexal ectopic, with serial beta-hCG monitoring to track resolution while continuing the intrauterine pregnancy
options.rationale: Methotrexate is contraindicated when a viable intrauterine pregnancy coexists with an ectopic (heterotopic pregnancy) because it would terminate the IUP; surgical management is required (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Expectant management with serial 48-hour beta-hCG and repeat transvaginal ultrasound in 1 week, given haemodynamic stability and the presence of a viable intrauterine pregnancy
options.rationale: Heterotopic pregnancy with a 30 mm adnexal mass, increasing pain, dizziness and free fluid carries a high rupture risk; expectant management is not appropriate. The beta-hCG trajectory is also uninterpretable because the IUP confounds it (RANZCOG).

options.text: Admit for observation and analgesia with 6-hourly observations, planning to operate only if she develops haemodynamic compromise
options.rationale: Watchful waiting in a haemodynamically stable but symptomatic heterotopic pregnancy with adnexal mass and free fluid is unsafe; rupture risk is high and definitive surgery should be planned urgently rather than reactively (RANZCOG).

options.text: Ultrasound-guided potassium chloride injection into the adnexal mass with continued expectant care of the intrauterine pregnancy
options.rationale: KCl injection is occasionally used in interstitial or cornual ectopics where surgical access is hazardous, but it is not first-line for a tubal heterotopic with rupture risk and prior salpingectomy; laparoscopic surgery remains the standard (RANZCOG).

options.text: Urgent laparoscopic salpingectomy of the left adnexal ectopic with intra-operative care to preserve the intrauterine pregnancy, plus anti-D if RhD negative
options.rationale: Heterotopic pregnancy (coexistent IUP and ectopic, much more common after ART) requires surgical treatment of the ectopic component because methotrexate would terminate the IUP. Laparoscopic salpingectomy is preferred for a complete tubal ectopic, particularly with prior contralateral salpingectomy; care is taken to minimise uterine manipulation and continue luteal support to protect the IUP. Anti-D 250 IU IM is given if RhD negative as this is a sensitising event pre-12 weeks (RANZCOG; RCOG; NBA).
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

explanation.summary: Heterotopic pregnancy (intrauterine plus ectopic) is rare in spontaneous conception (~1 in 30 000) but much more common after assisted reproductive technology (~1 in 100 to 1 in 500). Methotrexate is contraindicated because it would terminate the IUP; laparoscopic surgery is the treatment of choice, taking care to minimise uterine manipulation and continue luteal support.
key_points: Heterotopic incidence: ~1 in 30 000 spontaneous; ~1 in 100 to 500 with ART
hCG trajectory is uninterpretable when an IUP is present
Methotrexate contraindicated when a viable IUP coexists - it would terminate the IUP
Surgical treatment: laparoscopic salpingectomy (or salpingostomy) with minimal uterine handling, continue luteal support
Anti-D 250 IU IM if RhD negative; this is a sensitising event regardless of gestation
Suspect heterotopic in any post-ART pregnancy with pain and adnexal findings even when an IUP is visible
explanation.pearls: Always image both adnexae in any pregnant ART patient with pelvic pain, even after confirming an intrauterine pregnancy - the IUP does not exclude a coexistent ectopic.

sources.label: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin
